coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for the fluorescence detection of lactose in milk

coredata.dc:description: For lactose-intolerant patients, controlling lactose intake is crucial. To address this need, we developed carbon dot-based sensors for lactose detection in milk. Using citric acid and sulfamic acid as precursors, we synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) via hydrothermal method. The resulting N,S-CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and fluorescence properties, making them ideal for analytical applications. Lactose can be converted to hydrogen peroxide by the cascade enzyme method of lactase and glucose oxidase. Given the activity of hydrogen peroxide in the Fenton reaction and that the fluorescence of CDs can be significantly quenched by the Fenton reaction, a simple and convenient method for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactose was established. The strong oxidizing property of hydroxyl radicals produced in the Fenton reaction destroys the C-O bonds on the carboxylic acid groups of the CDs, which results in the quenched emission peak at 418 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs is linearly related to the concentration of hydrogen peroxide (0.1–5 μM), with a detection limit of 0.09 μM. For lactose in milk, a detection limit of 0.36 μM (0.5–22 μM) was obtained. The method for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactose based on the Fenton reaction and N, S co-doped CDs exhibits good stability, specificity, and selectivity. Moreover, the application potential of the method was verified in milk.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensing
Carbon dots
H2O2
Lactose
Food safety

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs; (B) The distribution of Particle size of CDs; (C,D) the fluorescence characteristics of the carbon point. UV–Vis absorption spectrum (a), fluorescence excitation spectrum (b) and emission spectrum (c); (E) FT-IR spectrum; (F) time-resolved spectrum; (G,H) XPS C1s spectrum before and after the addition of lactose.

(A, B) UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded upon the addition of GOx, β-Gal, lactose, and ferrous ions to the carbon point. (C) Illustrates the addition of lactose at different concentrations; (D) Fluorescence intensity corresponding to varying lactose concentrations.
The anti-interference ability (A, C) and selectivity (B, D) of lactose detected by carbon dots.
Illustrates the process of measuring lactose using CDs.
Comparison of performances of different methods for detection of lactose.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for the fluorescence detection of lactose in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Provincial Research Center of Light Industrial Optoelectronic Engineering and Technology, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Science, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Illustrates the process of measuring lactose using carbon dots.

For lactose-intolerant patients, controlling lactose intake is crucial. To address this need, we developed carbon dot-based sensors for lactose detection in milk. Using citric acid and sulfamic acid as precursors, we synthesized nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N,S-CDs) via hydrothermal method. The resulting N,S-CDs exhibit excellent water solubility and fluorescence properties, making them ideal for analytical applications. Lactose can be converted to hydrogen peroxide by the cascade enzyme method of lactase and glucose oxidase. Given the activity of hydrogen peroxide in the Fenton reaction and that the fluorescence of CDs can be significantly quenched by the Fenton reaction, a simple and convenient method for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactose was established. The strong oxidizing property of hydroxyl radicals produced in the Fenton reaction destroys the C-O bonds on the carboxylic acid groups of the CDs, which results in the quenched emission peak at 418 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs is linearly related to the concentration of hydrogen peroxide (0.1–5 μM), with a detection limit of 0.09 μM. For lactose in milk, a detection limit of 0.36 μM (0.5–22 μM) was obtained. The method for the detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactose based on the Fenton reaction and N, S co-doped CDs exhibits good stability, specificity, and selectivity. Moreover, the application potential of the method was verified in milk.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Lactose represents a substantial component of milk, contributing approximately 25 % of its total caloric content. It provides notable health benefits, such as a low glycemic index and reduced cariogenic potential relative to other sugars. In infants, lactose serves as a crucial nutrient, promoting the colonization of gut microbiota and providing protection against neonatal infections [1–3]. However, a significant proportion of the global population suffers from milk allergies and lactose intolerance. Lactose intolerance refers to the body's inability to hydrolyze lactose into its simpler forms for absorption, primarily due to the deficiency or complete absence of the lactase enzyme [4,5]. Hence, individuals with lactose intolerance should pay attention to their dietary habits on a regular basis. It is advisable for them to select lactose-free or low-lactose milk or milk powder as substitutes so as to ensure a sufficient supply of protein. In recent years, an increasing number of studies on the production of lactose-free and low-lactose milk have been carried out by dairy manufacturers. Identifying an efficient, economical and accurate method for testing lactose in low-lactose products is of great significance.

Traditionally, the reference method for quantifying lactose in cow's milk is high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6–8]. Although the results obtained by HPLC is accurate, the measurement of lactose is cumbersome and difficult. In contemporary detection methodologies, infrared spectroscopy and electrochemical sensing are commonly employed [9–11]. However, infrared spectroscopy is prohibitively expensive and unsuitable for field applications. The electrochemical method requires electrode modification, limiting its versatility [12,13]. Consequently, developing an efficient, cost-effective, and precise method for detecting lactose in low-lactose products holds significant importance[14–17].

Carbon dots, an emerging class of carbon-based nanomaterials, are characterized by their small size (typically below 10 nm), exceptional luminescent properties, and excellent biocompatibility. These attributes have enabled their broad applications across diverse fields, including biomedicine, diagnostics, and food science, demonstrating significant potential for further development. For instance, Ahmed Z. et al. engineered pH-sensitive ratiometric fluorescent probes based on blue- and red-emitting CDs for the ultrasensitive detection of L-asparaginase, achieving a detection limit of 6.95 U L−1. Similarly, Ramadan Ali et al. developed terbium-nitrogen co-doped CDs (Tb@N-CDs) coupled with α-thiocyanate-functionalized copper nanoclusters for ratiometric sensing of fluoroquinolone antibiotics, attaining a detection limit of 1.6 nM for ciprofloxacin with a linear range of 0.008–120 μM. Current synthetic strategies for CDs predominantly rely on bottom-up approaches, where molecular precursors (e.g., citric acid, urea, or pharmaceutical compounds) undergo controlled chemical transformations. Common methods include hydrothermal synthesis, microwave-assisted synthesis, and solvothermal synthesis. Among these, the hydrothermal method is widely favored due to its simplicity, high efficiency, and broad applicability.

In this paper, CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using citric acid and sulfamic acid. As shown in Scheme 1 , in an acidic environment and in the presence of Fe2+, the fluorescence of the CDs was quenched upon the addition of hydrogen peroxide. Building on this principle, lactose is enzymatically hydrolyzed into galactose and glucose by β-galactosidase, and subsequently, glucose oxidase catalyzes the conversion of glucose into hydrogen peroxide, enabling the detection of lactose. This method exhibits excellent stability, simplicity in synthesis, and low cost, offering a promising approach for lactose detection.

FeCl2·4H2O, Citric acid, lysine, valine, tryptophan, leucine, glutathion, glycine, L-phenylalanine, and ascorbic acid were all purchased from Meryer Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sulfamic acid, HCl were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Gox, β-Gal were all bought from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co.,Ltd(Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm) used throughout the experiments was obtained from a Labonova Smart ultrapure water system (Think-lab, Germany).

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were photographed by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were photographed by a GeminiSEM 500 (Zeiss AG Co Ltd, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were recorded on a D8 Advance diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed with Frontier infrared spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with ESCALAB 250Xi photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2600 ultra-violet spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The steady-state fluorescence spectra and the time-resolved spectra of 420 emission were measured by a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instrument, UK), and the time-resolved spectra of 550 nm were obtained with a self-built time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) system [18].

Weigh 0.15 g of citric acid and 0.25 g of sulfonic acid. Dissolve these compounds in 15 mL of deionized water under ultrasonic irradiation to ensure complete dissolution. Transfer the solution to a reactor and maintain at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling the sample to room temperature, dialyze the resultant solution against water for 24 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da, yielding CDs.

0.139 g of ferric chloride monohydrate was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water to prepare a 10 mM ferric chloride solution. The concentrated hydrochloric acid was diluted to a concentration of 1.5 mM. Subsequently, 2 mL of the prepared hydrochloric acid solution, 0.3 mL of ferrous chloride solution, 0.2 mL of CDs, 0.2 mL of deionized water, and 0.3 mL of varying concentrations of hydrogen peroxide solution were sequentially added to a 5 mL centrifuge tube. A 3 mL sample solution was then prepared, transferred to a cuvette, and analyzed using a fluorescence spectrometer to measure changes in fluorescence intensity of the CDs, thereby determining the concentration of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. S1)

To detect lactose concentration, a known quantity of lactose was dissolved in deionized water, followed by the addition of 0.5 g β-galactosidase. The mixture was heated on a magnetic stirring hotplate at 40 °C for 40 min. Next, 0.5 g glucose oxidase was added, and the mixture was further heated at 38 °C for 40 min. The resulting hydrogen peroxide solution was subjected to the aforementioned procedure to quantify hydrogen peroxide. This indirectly measured the lactose concentration.

During the lactose detection process, Fe2+ participates as a reactant in the Fenton reaction, and the effect of its concentration on the quenching of CDs fluorescence intensity was investigated. Solutions of ferrous chloride at concentrations of 1 μM, 10 μM, 100 μM, and 1 mM were selected for addition, and it was discovered that the CDs exhibited the highest degree of quenching at a concentration of 10 μM (Fig. S3). Additionally, the reaction time between lactose and the CDs was explored, as illustrated in Fig. S4, and it can be observed that the fluorescence of the CDs was quenched within 5 min. The synthesis conditions for CDs were systematically optimized by varying reaction parameters. As shown in Fig. S5, the optimal synthesis conditions were determined to be 180 °C for 6 h with a molar ratio of 3:10 (citric acid to sulfamic acid).

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was determined using a comparative method with quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the reference standard [19]. The absorption intensities of both the CD sample and the reference solution were measured at 332 nm, followed by recording their emission spectra under the corresponding excitation wavelength. The QY was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
ϕ =
ϕ s
·
I
I s
·
A s
A
·
η
2
η
s
2
where
ϕ
is the quantum yield,
I
represents the integrated fluorescence intensity,
A
denotes the absorbance at the excitation wavelength,
η
is the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscriptsrefers to the standard sample.

The morphologies of the CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images revealed that the CDs exhibited uniform spherical shapes with an average particle size of 4.18 nm and a lattice spacing of d = 0.237 nm, as demonstrated in Fig. 1 A and 1B. Furthermore, the fluorescence properties of the CDs were investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 1C. Curve a represents the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CD solution over the range of 200–700 nm, while curves b and c correspond to the excitation and emission spectra, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1C, the optimal excitation wavelength for the CDs is 332 nm, with an emission peak at 418 nm. A three-dimensional contour plot (Fig. 1D) was generated to illustrate the fluorescence excitation and emission intensity of the CDs. According to the three-dimensional fluorescence image, the emission peak position redshifted as the excitation wavelength increased, indicating that the CDs exhibit excitation-wavelength-dependent behavior and have non-uniform surface characteristics [20,21]. The quantum yield (QY) of the CDs was determined to be 10.4 % using quinine sulfate as the reference standard.

The characterization of CDs using Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy is illustrated in Fig. 1E. The results indicate that the C–H stretching vibration and O–H stretching vibration occur at 3126 cm−1 [22]. The CO stretching vibration is observed at 1724 cm−1 [23,24], and the C–H bending vibration along with the O–H in-plane bending vibration are present at 1400 cm−1 [25]. The carboxylic acid C–O single bond stretching vibration is identified at 1288 cm−1 [26], the C–C stretching vibration at 1174 cm−1.Additionally the carboxylic acid O–H out-of-plane bending vibration at 886 cm−1 [22], and the N–H out-of-plane bending vibration at 578 cm−1 [27]. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was conducted before and after the addition of hydrogen peroxide, revealing that the intensities at 1724 cm−1, 1288 cm−1, and 886 cm−1 significantly decreased following the introduction of hydrogen peroxide, with the most pronounced change occurring at 1288 cm−1. This suggests that upon the addition of hydrogen peroxide, hydroxyl radicals were generated, leading to the oxidation of carboxylic acid groups on the CDs. Consequently, this process resulted in structural damage to the CDs and fluorescence quenching. The XPS analysis results are presented in Fig. 1G and 1H. The C1s spectrum exhibit absorption peaks at 284.4, 286.43, and 289.06 eV, corresponding to C–C, C-O, and COOR bonds, respectively [28–33]. These findings confirm the successful synthesis of CDs. Upon the addition of hydrogen peroxide, a reduction in the absorption peaks associated with CO and C–O bonds in carboxylic acids is observed. This provides further evidence that the Fenton reaction degrades carboxylic acid groups on CDs.

Additionally, the fluorescence lifetime of CDs was measured before and after the introduction of hydrogen peroxide. The results indicate an 8.6 % decrease in fluorescence lifetime following the addition of hydrogen peroxide (Fig. 1F), suggesting significant attenuation. This observation implies dynamic quenching and corroborates the degradation of carboxyl functional groups as a result of the reaction [34].

As depicted in Fig. 2 A and B, β-galactosidase, glucose oxidase, lactose, and ferrous chloride were sequentially added to the CDs, and it was noted that the absorption spectrum of the CDs solution underwent a change, and the fluorescence of the corresponding CDs was quenched, only when lactose, enzyme, and ferrous chloride were simultaneously introduced to the CDs. This observation suggests that the quenching of CDs fluorescence is attributed to the Fenton reaction. Further, we examined the influence of lactose concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the system, and as illustrated in Fig. 2C and D, a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was observed with the increase in lactose concentration. A good linear relationship was obtained when the lactose concentration ranged from 0.5 to 22 μM. The linear equation is Y = −2480.51x + 159918.1 (R = 0.99016), where x represents the lactose concentration. The detection limit (LOD, S/N = 3) [35]was determined to be 0.36 μM. Superior to most lactose tests, Table 1 .

To evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference ability of CDs in lactose detection, eight amino acids and nutrients frequently occurring in milk were added to the lactose-based system, as depicted in Fig. 3 . These substances included lysine (Lys), valine (Val), tryptophan (Try), leucine (Leu), glutathione (GSH), glycine (Gly), L-phenylalanine (L-phe), and ascorbic acid, all at a uniform concentration of 10 mM. As evidenced in Fig. 3A and 3B, lactose alone is capable of causing quenching of CDs. In contrast, other substances fail to induce such an effect, suggesting negligible interference. This result implies that the synthesized CDs fulfill the criteria necessary for practical application in detection.

To assess the detection performance of CDs for lactose in actual samples, the lactose content in lactose-free milk was determined via the standard addition recovery method, where distinct concentrations of lactose were introduced into the milk. The experimental procedure was conducted as follows: The milk sample was initially diluted 10-fold with deionized water, followed by the addition of an equal volume of acetone. After standing for a predetermined duration, the mixture was centrifuged using a high-speed centrifuge (12,000 rpm for 10 min) to separate the supernatant. Subsequently, known concentrations of lactose (5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 μM) were spiked into the processed samples to perform recovery experiments. The outcomes are presented in Table 2 . The recoveries ranged from 96.6 % to 106.9 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD) values less than 3 %. This indicates that the CDs possess favorable selectivity and feasibility, demonstrating good practical applicability.

In this paper, citric acid and sulfamic acid were employed as precursors for the synthesis of CDs, and the reaction between CDs and hydroxyl radicals generated via the Fenton reaction disrupted the carboxyl structure of CDs, leading to fluorescence quenching. Consequently, such CDs can be utilized for highly sensitive detection of hydrogen peroxide and lactose. The detection limit of lactose in milk was found to be 0.36 μM (0.5–22 μM). The satisfactory recoveries in lactose-free milk indicate that the established method based on CDs offers the advantages of low cost, rapid reaction rate, high sensitivity, and good selectivity. This work not only proposes a novel method for the specific and highly sensitive detection of lactose in milk, which is essential for ensuring the well-being of lactose-intolerant patients, but also broadens the application scope of CDs in sensors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China [62375112].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126357.
